stem: Consider the following procedure, where \( c \) is initialized to 0: \[ \textbf{for } i = 1 \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \quad \textbf{for } j = 1 \textbf{ to } i: \quad c \leftarrow c + 1 \] What is the value of \( c \) after the procedure finishes?
solution: For each \( i \) from 1 to [[n]], the inner loop runs \( i \) times, so \( c = 1 + 2 + \cdots + [[n]] = \frac{[[n]]([[n]]+1)}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes the inner loop runs [[n]] times for every \( i \), giving \( n^{2} \).
This sums \( 1 \) through \( [[n]]-1 \), dropping the last pass of the inner loop.
This forgets to divide the sum by 2.
This overcounts by an extra [[n]] increments.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Consider the following procedure, where \( c \) is initialized to 0: \[ \textbf{for } i = 1 \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \quad \textbf{for } j = 1 \textbf{ to } [[m]]: \quad c \leftarrow c + 1 \] What is the value of \( c \) after the procedure finishes?
solution: Since the inner loop always runs the full [[m]] passes regardless of \( i \), the total count is \( [[n]] \times [[m]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two bounds instead of multiplying them.
This mistakenly treats the inner bound as depending on \( i \), as in a triangular count.
This mistakenly treats the outer bound as depending on \( j \), as in a triangular count.
This confuses repeated multiplication with exponentiation.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Consider the following procedure, where \( c \) is initialized to 0: \[ \textbf{for } i = 1, 1+[[k]], 1+2\cdot[[k]], \ldots \textbf{ while } i \le [[n]]: \quad c \leftarrow c + 1 \] That is, \( i \) starts at 1 and increases by [[k]] each pass as long as \( i \le [[n]] \). What is the value of \( c \) after the procedure finishes?
solution: The values taken by \( i \) are \( 1, 1+[[k]], 1+2[[k]], \ldots \), i.e. \( i = 1 + t\cdot[[k]] \) for \( t = 0, 1, 2, \ldots \); the last pass has the largest \( t \) with \( 1+t[[k]] \le [[n]] \), so there are \( \lfloor ([[n]]-1)/[[k]] \rfloor + 1 = [[answer]] \) passes.
distractor_rationales: This divides \( n \) directly by the step instead of accounting for starting the sequence at \( i=1 \).
This forgets to add the initial pass at \( i=1 \).
This leaves the count as a raw (non-integer) ratio instead of the correct floor-based formula.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by two from the correct total.

stem: Consider the following procedure, where \( c \) is initialized to 0: \[ \textbf{for } i = 1 \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \quad \textbf{for } j = 1 \textbf{ to } i: \quad \textbf{for } k = 1 \textbf{ to } j: \quad c \leftarrow c + 1 \] What is the value of \( c \) after the procedure finishes?
solution: The triple counts exactly the non-decreasing triples \( 1 \le k \le j \le i \le [[n]] \), and the number of such triples is \( \binom{[[n]]+2}{3} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes all three loops run the full [[n]] passes, ignoring that the inner bounds shrink.
This uses the ordinary (non-binomial) cubic-growth approximation, which is close but not exact.
This uses \( \binom{n+1}{3} \), off by one in the binomial's upper index.
This computes only the two-level triangular count, forgetting the third nested loop.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Consider the following procedure, where \( c \) is initialized to 0: \[ \textbf{for } i = 1 \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \quad \textbf{for } j = i+1 \textbf{ to } [[n]]: \quad \textbf{if } i+j \bmod 2 = 0: \quad c \leftarrow c + 1 \] What is the value of \( c \) after the procedure finishes? (Here \( i+j \bmod 2 = 0 \) means \( i \) and \( j \) have the same parity.)
solution: Among \( 1,\ldots,[[n]] \) there are \( \lceil [[n]]/2 \rceil \) odd numbers and \( \lfloor [[n]]/2 \rfloor \) even numbers; a pair \( i<j \) is counted exactly when both are odd or both are even, giving \( \binom{\lceil [[n]]/2 \rceil}{2} + \binom{\lfloor [[n]]/2 \rfloor}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts every pair, ignoring the same-parity condition entirely.
This guesses that exactly half of all pairs match the condition, which is not exact for general [[n]].
This counts only the odd-odd pairs, forgetting the even-even pairs.
This counts only the even-even pairs, forgetting the odd-odd pairs.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.
